request.question: What do the Lost McLeod Mine and the Colomac Mine have in common?

title: Lost Lemon Mine
Lost McLeod Mine
Pebble Mine
Colomac Mine
Colomac Airport
Wheelbarrow Mine
Ryan House and Lost Horse Well
Pitt Lake's lost gold mine
Jolly Jack's Lost Mine
Wopkaimin people
sentences: The Lost Lemon Mine is a legendary lost mine said to be located in the Canadian Province of Alberta.

The story has been retold in countless books with the authoritative version being "The Lost Lemon Mine" by Tom Primrose.The story first appeared in the 1946 edition of the "Alberta Folklore Quarterly" and later in magazines such as "Canada West".
The Lost Lemon Mine has also been featured in television documentary series "Northern Mysteries", and worked into the plot of "The Final Sacrifice".
Countless people have searched for the mine.
A number of searchers have never returned.
Lost McLeod Mine is a legend of a lost mine in the Northwest Territories of Canada.
The story has been featured in many books and magazines.
The events in the legend have led to geographic locations in the Northwest Territories being named Headless Valley and Headless Creek located in Nahanni National Park Reserve.
A creek called Sheep Creek was renamed McCleod Creek in honour of the Mcleod Brothers.
20 people have lost their lives searching for The Lost McLeod Mine.

Pebble Mine is the common name of a mineral exploration project investigating a very large porphyry copper, gold, and molybdenum mineral deposit in the Bristol Bay region of Southwest Alaska, near Lake Iliamna and Lake Clark.
The proposal to mine the ore deposit, using large-scale operations and infrastructure, is controversial.
Proponents argue that the mine will create jobs, provide tax revenue to the state of Alaska, and reduce American dependence on foreign sources of raw materials.

Opponents argue that the mine would adversely affect the entire Bristol Bay watershed; and that the possible consequences to fish populations, when mining effluents escape planned containments, are simply too great of a risk.
Much of this debate concerns the tentative plan to impound large amounts of water, waste rock, and mine tailings behind several earthen dams at the mine site.
The project is on hold after the loss of funding partners in 2013.
The Colomac Mine was a privately owned and operated open pit gold mine located 220 km northwest of Yellowknife in the Northwest Territories in Canada .
The Colomac mine operated between 1990–1992, and 1994–1997.
It was operated by Neptune Resources Limited that had little success in making a profit during its operation.
In 1994, the mine had reopened under Royal Oak Mines Inc.
Both Neptune Resources and Royal Oak Mines where both owned and operated by Peggy Witte.
Due to low gold prices and high cost of mining, Royal Oak Mines was forced into bankruptcy.
The Federal Government of Canada became owners of the mine, along with the related environmental issues.
A major cleanup effort is under way to prevent the mine from polluting the environment, but this might be too late at this stage.
This mine is now owned and controlled by the Indigenous and Northern Affairs department of the Federal government, while Public Works and Government services is the current contracting authority.
Colomac Airport (TC LID: CFY8) , was located near Colomac Mine in the Northwest Territories, Canada where caribou may be found on the runway.
Prior permission was required to land except in the case of an emergency.
The lost Wheelbarrow Mine is claimed to have been located about 10 mi from Potlatch in Latah County, Idaho.
The reported location is 3,871 feet above sea level, at coordinates 46.9975° N, 116.7833° W.
The mine, believed to have been dug prior to 1890, was said to have produced $20,000 in gold, before a falling-out between its operators led to its abandonment.
One of the operators later returned to the area to find the mine again, but was unsuccessful.
In June 1939, miners with the Fitsum Mining company uncovered an abandoned mine matching the description of the Wheelbarrow mine, containing human bone presumed to belong to the other operator.
The Ryan House and Lost Horse Well are historic ruins in Joshua Tree National Park, California, United States.
It was established by the family of J.D. Ryan, the later developers of the Lost Horse Mine, which became the most profitable mine in the area.
The Lost Horse Well at the Ryan Ranch supplied water to the Lost Horse Mine, 3 mi south and 750 ft , by pipeline.

Pitt Lake's Lost Gold Mine is a legendary lost mine said to be near Pitt Lake, British Columbia, Canada, the supposed wealth of which has held the imagination of people worldwide for more than a century.
Ever since the years of the Fraser Canyon Gold Rush prospectors and adventurers have been looking for the mine and gold-rush rumors have evolved into legends repeated and enriched over time.
The mysterious riches are known as Slumach’s Lost Mine, or Lost Creek Mine.
Jolly Jack's Lost Mine is a legend about a hidden placer mine supposed to be located in the Boundary Country of British Columbia.
The mine has been written about by local historians and various magazines.
Many have searched for the lost placer mine of Jolly Jack.
It is a local legend in the Boundary Country.
The Wopkaimin are a small aboriginal tribe that lives in the remote Star Mountains in western Papua New Guinea in what is known as the Fly River socio-ecological region.
The tribe speaks Faiwol.
The Ok Tedi Mine, the third largest open pit copper and gold mine in the world is located in their traditional territory.
Before the coming of the mine with construction starting in 1981 the Wopkaimin lived in a subsistence economy.
The mine severely impacted the tribe, totally disrupting their traditional patterns of life.
For one thing, Tabubil a town of 12,500 to house mine workers was built in the midst of their territory.
Work for wages was available to tribal members, but only at the unskilled level and not on a regular basis.

The Wopkaimin along with many other ethnic groups living in the area now live on a rotating basis between the town of Tabubil, roadside villages along the Kiunga-Tabubil Highway, and in villages away from the mine as jobs are lost or become available.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (context or passages) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection is mandatory and must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to the extractive rules below. Use the extractor that matches the question intent and only apply an extractor when the summaries present the required adjacent or verbatim phrasing.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind").
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

response.hop1_query: What do the Lost McLeod Mine and the Colomac Mine have in common?
hop1_docs: Lost McLeod Mine | Lost McLeod Mine is a legend of a lost mine in the Northwest Territories of Canada.  The story has been featured in many books and magazines.  The events in the legend have led to geographic locations in the Northwest Territories being named Headless Valley and Headless Creek located in Nahanni National Park Reserve.  A creek called Sheep Creek was renamed McCleod Creek in honour of the Mcleod Brothers.  20 people have lost their lives searching for The Lost McLeod Mine.

Colomac Mine | The Colomac Mine was a privately owned and operated open pit gold mine located 220 km northwest of Yellowknife in the Northwest Territories in Canada .  The Colomac mine operated between 1990–1992, and 1994–1997.  It was operated by Neptune Resources Limited that had little success in making a profit during its operation.  In 1994, the mine had reopened under Royal Oak Mines Inc.  Both Neptune Resources and Royal Oak Mines where both owned and operated by Peggy Witte.  Due to low gold prices and high cost of mining, Royal Oak Mines was forced into bankruptcy.  The Federal Government of Canada became owners of the mine, along with the related environmental issues.  A major cleanup effort is under way to prevent the mine from polluting the environment, but this might be too late at this stage.  This mine is now owned and controlled by the Indigenous and Northern Affairs department of the Federal government, while Public Works and Government services is the current contracting authority.

Colomac Airport | Colomac Airport (TC LID: CFY8) , was located near Colomac Mine in the Northwest Territories, Canada where caribou may be found on the runway.  Prior permission was required to land except in the case of an emergency.
I Wonder What They're Doing To-Night (Your Girl and Mine) | I Wonder What They're Doing To-Night (Your Girl and Mine)

List of lost mines | Lost mines are a popular form of lost treasure legend.  The mine involved is usually of a high-value commodity such as gold, silver or diamonds.  Often there is a map (sometimes called a "waybill") purportedly showing the location of the mine.  Common reasons given for the mines being lost include:

Pitt Lake's lost gold mine | Pitt Lake's Lost Gold Mine is a legendary lost mine said to be near Pitt Lake, British Columbia, Canada, the supposed wealth of which has held the imagination of people worldwide for more than a century.  Ever since the years of the Fraser Canyon Gold Rush prospectors and adventurers have been looking for the mine and gold-rush rumors have evolved into legends repeated and enriched over time.  The mysterious riches are known as Slumach’s Lost Mine, or Lost Creek Mine.

Lost Dutch Oven Mine | One of the most famous lost mines in California is known as the Lost Dutch Oven mine.  There are several versions of the story, as is the case with most lost mine legends, perhaps the best known is the one with Tom Schofield, a railroad worker, decided to do some prospecting in his time off.  According to this version, while prospecting in the Clipper Mountains, northwest of Essex (in San Bernardino county), he found an old stone house.  As the house appeared to be long abandoned, he continued on.  Several miles further in his travels he found a spring.  Near the spring he spotted an old trail.  Following the trail, it led him up over a hill where he spotted a huge boulder that was split in two.  The path went through this split boulder, and into what he believed was an ancient Spanish camp.
hop1_titles: I Wonder What They're Doing To-Night (Your Girl and Mine)

- Lost McLeod Mine: "legend of a lost mine in the Northwest Territories of Canada." ; "Headless Valley and Headless Creek located in Nahanni National Park Reserve." ; "Sheep Creek was renamed McCleod Creek." ; "20 people have lost their lives searching for The Lost McLeod Mine."
- Colomac Mine: "open pit gold mine located 220 km northwest of Yellowknife in the Northwest Territories in Canada." ; "operated between 1990–1992, and 1994–1997." ; "operated by Neptune Resources Limited" ; "reopened under Royal Oak Mines Inc. in 1994."
- Ambiguity / missing fact: The passages do not state whether the Lost McLeod Mine is a gold (or other-commodity) mine — "commodity (gold?) for Lost McLeod" is missing. Bridge entity to resolve: Lost McLeod Mine page/entry.
- Next-hop retrievals (priority order):
  1. Fetch "Lost McLeod Mine" full article — inspect lede/infobox and "History" or "Legend" sections to confirm commodity and precise location.
  2. Fetch "Colomac Mine" full article — inspect lede/infobox and "Overview"/"History" sections to confirm gold-mine details (dates/operators already cited).
  3. Search keywords: "Lost McLeod Mine gold", "Lost McLeod Nahanni National Park commodity", and "Lost McLeod Mine gold Northwest Territories".
hop2_docs: Lost McLeod Mine | Lost McLeod Mine is a legend of a lost mine in the Northwest Territories of Canada.  The story has been featured in many books and magazines.  The events in the legend have led to geographic locations in the Northwest Territories being named Headless Valley and Headless Creek located in Nahanni National Park Reserve.  A creek called Sheep Creek was renamed McCleod Creek in honour of the Mcleod Brothers.  20 people have lost their lives searching for The Lost McLeod Mine.

List of lost mines | Lost mines are a popular form of lost treasure legend.  The mine involved is usually of a high-value commodity such as gold, silver or diamonds.  Often there is a map (sometimes called a "waybill") purportedly showing the location of the mine.  Common reasons given for the mines being lost include:

Soft commodity | Soft commodities, or softs, are commodities such as coffee, cocoa, sugar, corn, wheat, soybean, fruit and livestock.  The term generally refers to commodities that are grown, rather than mined; the latter (such as oil, copper and gold) are known as hard commodities.

Nicholas McLeod | Nicholas McLeod (fl.  1868–1889), in some accounts called Norman McLeod, was a native of the Isle of Skye, Scotland known for his theory that the Japanese people descended from the Lost Tribes of Israel.

McLeod, Oregon | McLeod is an unincorporated community in Jackson County, Oregon, United States.  It lies at the confluence of Big Butte Creek with the Rogue River just downstream of Lost Creek Lake.  It is along Oregon Route 234 between Shady Cove and Prospect.  The community was named after its 1910 settler William R. McLeod.

The Mining Journal (trade magazine) | The Mining Journal is a global business magazine and online media publication for the mining sector, written in English, covering daily developments within major mining companies, commodities finance markets, investment opportunities in mining, geological exploration, mining technology and the business of operating mines.

Commodity market | A commodity market is a market that trades in primary economic sector rather than manufactured products.  Soft commodities are agricultural products such as wheat, coffee, cocoa, fruit and sugar.  Hard commodities are mined, such as gold and oil.  Investors access about 50 major commodity markets worldwide with purely financial transactions increasingly outnumbering physical trades in which goods are delivered.  Futures contracts are the oldest way of investing in commodities.  Futures are secured by physical assets.  Commodity markets can include physical trading and derivatives trading using spot prices, forwards, futures, and options on futures.  Farmers have used a simple form of derivative trading in the commodity market for centuries for price risk management.
response.summary_2: Primary topics: Lost McLeod Mine; Colomac Mine — mine(s) in the Northwest Territories, Canada. Lost McLeod Mine is a legendary lost mine associated with Nahanni National Park Reserve (Headless Valley and Headless Creek) and has been linked to about 20 deaths of people who searched for it.
response.answer: mine(s) in the Northwest Territories, Canada
